ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 covers spring roll as a food product; Thai-style deep-fried variant is a close exact match at the category level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP for spring rolls is an exact category match; Thai deep-fried preparation is a subtype within this category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for spring roll as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 4 pieces of Thai crispy spring rolls, weighing approximately 160 g, as typically served as a starter or snack portion at a Thai restaurant or food stall.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Spring Rolls, Vegetable (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods) adapted for deep-fried Thai-style preparation; cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for fried spring roll category
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai-style deep-fried spring roll as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried spring roll / egg roll entries (FDC ID 169736 and related) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore fried spring roll category data. Input per_100g values (energy 281 kcal, protein 7.5 g, fat 18.75 g, carbohydrate 21.88 g) are consistent with the USDA/HPB adapted range for deep-fried vegetable-and-noodle spring rolls and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where the adapted source does not provide disaggregated values.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — Spring Roll / Egg Roll analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style deep-fried spring roll in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food: wheat-wrapper deep-fried spring roll / egg roll. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (0–55). gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 35.0) / 100 = 19.3 g, where carbohydrate per 160 g serving = 21.88 × 1.6 = 35.0 g. GL of 19.3 is classified as high GL (>20 borderline). Estimation basis documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 406 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 4-piece serving (160 g) delivers approximately 650 mg sodium, representing 28–32% of the 2000 mg daily limit, with minimal potassium or fibre offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit Thai crispy spring rolls to 1–2 pieces per occasion, request reduced-sodium dipping sauce, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the sodium load; regular consumption is not recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat wrappers and glass noodles contribute a GI of 55 (low boundary) with a glycemic load of 19.3 per serving; deep-frying adds 18.75 g fat/100 g which impairs postprandial insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat Thai crispy spring rolls as an occasional food, limit to 1–2 pieces, monitor postprandial blood glucose, and prefer baked or steamed alternatives to reduce both glycemic load and fat-driven insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.13 g/100 g from deep-frying, combined with sodium at 406 mg/100 g, present a composite cardiovascular risk profile; a 160 g serving delivers 8.0 g saturated fat and approximately 650 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of deep-fried spring rolls; if consumed, limit to 1–2 pieces infrequently, choose preparations using heart-healthy oils, and balance the meal with fibre-rich, low-sodium foods to mitigate arterial and lipid risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and the presence of trans fat at 0.13 g/100 g amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; both are hard disqualifiers for RA dietary management.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried spring rolls due to AGE generation and trans fat content; baked or steamed spring rolls with anti-inflammatory fillings are a preferred alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Crispy Spring Roll (ปอเปี๊ยะทอดไทย; 炸春卷) is a deep-fried snack consisting of thin wheat-based pastry wrappers filled with a savoury mixture of shredded cabbage, julienned carrot, glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli), wood-ear mushrooms, and optionally minced pork or chicken, seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce. The rolls are sealed and submerged in hot vegetable oil until the exterior is golden brown and shatteringly crisp, while the interior remains moist and tender. Texture contrast between the crunchy shell and soft filling is the defining sensory characteristic. In Singapore and across Southeast Asia, Thai-style spring rolls are commonly served as a starter, street snack, or side dish, often accompanied by sweet chilli sauce or plum sauce. Nutritionally, a 4-piece serving (160 g) provides approximately 450 kcal, 12 g protein, 30 g fat, and 35 g carbohydrate. The sodium content of approximately 650 mg per serving is clinically significant for hypertension management. The GI of 55 (low boundary per University of Sydney classification) and glycemic load of 19.3 per serving make portion control important for individuals with type 2 diabetes. Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.13 g/100 g are relevant cardiovascular risk factors. Deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products, which are pro-inflammatory and of concern for rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans when meat is included, and contains gluten, soy, and shellfish allergens from the wrapper and seasoning ingredients.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai-style deep-fried spring roll as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried spring roll / egg roll entries cross-referenced with HPB Singapore fried spring roll category data. Input values are consistent with the adapted range and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match and the composite nature of the dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: disaggregated fatty acid profile, full B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for deep-fried Thai-style spring roll are not available as a composite dish entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. The ethnic_tags in the input ('Curry', 'Pad Thai') appear to be erroneous metadata from the source platform and have been removed; the dish is a spring roll, not a curry or Pad Thai preparation. Trans fat at 0.13 g/100 g is retained from the input; this is consistent with residual trans fat from commercial frying oils. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (55 × 35.0) / 100 = 19.3, where 35.0 g carbohydrate per 160 g serving = 21.88 × 1.6. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI Database boundary definition (low GI = 55 or below); the previous draft incorrectly assigned 'medium' — corrected in this iteration. The dish is classified as Thai cuisine served in Singapore context; multilingual names cover Thai (mandatory), Chinese (present in original dish name), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as per Singaporean serving context.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index of 55. Per the University of Sydney GI Database, the low GI boundary is 0–55 inclusive, meaning GI 55 is classified as 'low', not 'medium'. Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect the corrected GI category. Updated notes field to document the correction. This resolves the q4_glycemic_completeness failure identified by the validator in iteration 2.
